question: What is the Arabic term for 'Subject' in a nominal sentence?
explanation: مُبْتَدَأٌ (Mubtada) is the subject/topic of a nominal sentence — it is what we are talking about.

question: What is the Arabic term for 'Predicate' — the information given about the subject?
explanation: خَبَرٌ (Khabar) is the predicate — it provides information about the Mubtada.

question: Which ending do BOTH the Mubtada and the Khabar always carry?
explanation: Both Mubtada and Khabar are in the Rafa' state — they end with dammah (ُ) or tanween dammah (ٌ).

question: What is the FIRST step when translating 'Khalid is brave' into Arabic?
options: Add ة to make it feminine
explanation: Step 1: Remove 'is'. Step 2: Translate the words. Step 3: Give both words dammah/tanween dammah.

question: Which of the following is the correct Arabic translation of 'Haamid is pious'?
explanation: حَامِدٌ (Mubtada) + صَالِحٌ (Khabar) — both with tanween dammah (ٌ). No alif-laam needed since Haamid is a proper name.

question: When alif-laam (ال) is added to a word that has tanween, what happens to the tanween?
options: Tanween drops and becomes a single vowel
explanation: When alif-laam is added, tanween drops and becomes a single dammah, kasrah, or fathah. E.g., رَجُلٌ → الرَّجُلُ.

question: In the sentence 'الْبِنْتُ عَالِمَةٌ', why does عَالِم have ة at the end?
options: Because البنت (girl) is feminine, so the Khabar must also be feminine
No special reason — ة is always added
explanation: If the Mubtada is feminine, the Khabar must also agree in gender. البِنتُ = girl (feminine) → عَالِمَةٌ.

question: How do you make the word صَالِحٌ (pious, masculine) into its feminine form?
options: Add the round taa (ة) to the end → صَالِحَةٌ
explanation: To make any adjective feminine in Arabic, add the round taa (ة) to the end: صَالِحٌ → صَالِحَةٌ.

question: The Mubtada of a nominal sentence is always a...
options: Definite noun (proper name or with alif-laam)
explanation: The Mubtada must be definite — either a proper name (like Mahmood) or a common noun with alif-laam (like الرَّجُلُ).

question: What does alif-laam (ال) primarily indicate in Arabic?
explanation: Alif-laam is the definite article — like 'the' in English. رَجُلٌ = a man; الرَّجُلُ = the man.

question: Identify the Mubtada in: الْقَائِدُ شُجَاعٌ
explanation: الْقَائِدُ (the leader) is the Mubtada — it comes first and is definite. شُجَاعٌ (brave) is the Khabar.

question: Identify the Khabar in: مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولٌ
explanation: رَسُولٌ (messenger) is the Khabar — it tells us what Muhammad is. مُحَمَّدٌ is the Mubtada.

question: Which sentence correctly says 'The woman is knowledgeable' in Arabic?
explanation: الْمَرْأَةُ (the woman) is feminine, so the Khabar عَالِمٌ must become عَالِمَةٌ (with ة).

question: In the sentence 'الْبَيْتُ كَبِيرٌ', what is the Khabar?
explanation: كَبِيرٌ (large) is the Khabar — it tells us information about the Mubtada (الْبَيْتُ = the house).

question: What does اللَّهُ رَبٌّ mean in English?
explanation: اللَّهُ (Allah) is the Mubtada; رَبٌّ (Lord) is the Khabar. Together: 'Allah is (the) Lord.'

question: What does الْإِنْسَانُ عَبْدٌ mean in English?
options: Mankind is a slave (servant of Allah)
explanation: الْإِنْسَانُ (mankind/human) is Mubtada; عَبْدٌ (slave/servant) is Khabar: 'Mankind is a slave/servant.'

question: Which is the correct Arabic for 'The path is straight'?
explanation: الصِّرَاطُ (the path, Mubtada) + مُسْتَقِيمٌ (straight, Khabar). Path is masculine so no ة needed.

question: What does أَبٌ mean?
explanation: أَبٌ (abun) = father. Its plural is آبَاءٌ.

question: What does أُمٌّ mean?
explanation: أُمٌّ (ummun) = mother. Its plural is أُمَّهَاتٌ.

question: What does أَخٌ mean?
explanation: أَخٌ (akhun) = brother. Its plural is إِخْوَةٌ.

question: What does أُخْتٌ mean?
explanation: أُخْتٌ (ukhtun) = sister. Its plural is أَخَوَاتٌ.

question: What does عَمٌّ mean?
explanation: عَمٌّ (ʿammun) = paternal uncle (father's brother). Maternal uncle is خَالٌ.

question: What does خَالٌ mean?
explanation: خَالٌ (khālun) = maternal uncle (mother's brother). Paternal uncle is عَمٌّ.

question: What does جَدٌّ mean?
explanation: جَدٌّ (jaddun) = grandfather. Its plural is أَجْدَادٌ. Grandmother is جَدَّةٌ.

question: What does حَفِيدٌ mean?
explanation: حَفِيدٌ (ḥafīdun) = grandson. Its plural is أَحْفَادٌ. Granddaughter is حَفِيدَةٌ.

question: What does طِفْلٌ mean?
explanation: طِفْلٌ (ṭiflun) = male child, infant, toddler. Plural: أَطْفَالٌ. Feminine: طِفْلَةٌ.

question: What does نَبِيٌّ mean?
explanation: نَبِيٌّ (nabiyyun) = prophet. Plural: أَنْبِيَاءٌ. Messenger is رَسُولٌ.

question: What does عَبْدٌ mean?
explanation: عَبْدٌ (ʿabdun) = slave, servant. Plural: عِبَادٌ. It often refers to a servant of Allah.

question: What does قَائِدٌ mean?
explanation: قَائِدٌ (qā'idun) = leader, commander. Plural: قَادَةٌ.

question: What does عَالِمٌ mean?
explanation: عَالِمٌ (ʿālimun) = knowledgeable, scholar. Plural: عُلَمَاءٌ.

question: What does صَالِحٌ mean?
explanation: صَالِحٌ (ṣāliḥun) = pious, righteous. It is both an adjective and a proper name.

question: What does صَادِقٌ mean?
explanation: صَادِقٌ (ṣādiqun) = truthful, honest. Plural: صَادِقُونَ.

question: What does شُجَاعٌ mean?
explanation: شُجَاعٌ (shujāʿun) = brave, courageous.

question: What does كَرِيمٌ mean?
explanation: كَرِيمٌ (karīmun) = generous, noble. Plural: كِرَامٌ.

question: What does ذَكِيٌّ mean?
explanation: ذَكِيٌّ (dhakiyyun) = intelligent, smart. Plural: أَذْكِيَاءٌ.

question: What does مُجْتَهِدٌ mean?
explanation: مُجْتَهِدٌ (mujtahidun) = hardworking, diligent. Plural: مُجْتَهِدُونَ.

question: What does رَحِيمٌ mean?
explanation: رَحِيمٌ (raḥīmun) = merciful. One of the Names of Allah: الرَّحِيمُ.

question: What does مَاءٌ mean?
explanation: مَاءٌ (mā'un) = water. Plural: مِيَاهٌ.

question: What does ثَلْجٌ mean?
explanation: ثَلْجٌ (thaljun) = ice, snow.

question: What does قَوِيٌّ mean?
explanation: قَوِيٌّ (qawiyyun) = strong. Its opposite is ضَعِيفٌ (weak). Plural: أَقْوِيَاءٌ.

question: What does ضَعِيفٌ mean?
explanation: ضَعِيفٌ (ḍaʿīfun) = weak. Its opposite is قَوِيٌّ (strong). Plural: ضُعَفَاءٌ.

question: What does جَمِيلٌ mean?
explanation: جَمِيلٌ (jamīlun) = beautiful.

question: Which Arabic word means 'son'?
explanation: ابْنٌ (ibnun) = son. وَلَدٌ = boy/child; أَخٌ = brother; حَفِيدٌ = grandson.

question: Which Arabic word means 'daughter / girl'?
explanation: بِنْتٌ (bintun) = daughter, girl. Plural: بَنَاتٌ.

question: Which Arabic word means 'man'?
explanation: رَجُلٌ (rajulun) = man. Plural: رِجَالٌ.

question: Which Arabic word means 'worshipper'?
explanation: عَابِدٌ (ʿābidun) = worshipper. Plural: عُبَّادٌ. عَبْدٌ = slave/servant; عَالِمٌ = scholar; عَاقِلٌ = wise.

question: Which Arabic word means 'Muslim'?
explanation: مُسْلِمٌ (muslimun) = Muslim. Plural: مُسْلِمُونَ.

question: Which Arabic word means 'small'?
explanation: صَغِيرٌ (ṣaghīrun) = small. Its opposite is كَبِيرٌ (large).

question: Which Arabic word means 'cold'?
explanation: بَارِدٌ (bāridun) = cold. It appeared in Lesson 1 describing: 'The ice is cold' (الثَّلْجُ بَارِدٌ).

question: Which Arabic word means 'sweet'?
explanation: عَذْبٌ (ʿadhbun) = sweet. E.g., الْمَاءُ عَذْبٌ = The water is sweet.

question: Translate into Arabic: 'The man is strong.'
explanation: الرَّجُلُ (the man, definite Mubtada) + قَوِيٌّ (strong, Khabar). Both carry dammah/tanween dammah.

question: Translate into Arabic: 'The child is weak.'
explanation: الطِّفْلُ (the child, M) + ضَعِيفٌ (weak). طِفْل is masculine so no ة on the khabar.

question: Translate into Arabic: 'The sister is beautiful.'
explanation: الْأُخْتُ (the sister) is feminine → Khabar must be جَمِيلَةٌ (with ة). Both take dammah.

question: What does الصِّرَاطُ مُسْتَقِيمٌ mean?
explanation: Mubtada-Khabar sentence: الصِّرَاطُ (the path) + مُسْتَقِيمٌ (straight) = 'The path is straight.'

question: What does السَّاعَةُ آتِيَةٌ mean?
options: The Hour (Day of Judgment) is coming
explanation: السَّاعَةُ = the Hour (Day of Resurrection); آتِيَةٌ = coming (feminine Khabar matching the feminine Mubtada).

question: In the idaafah construction, what is the Mudaaf?
explanation: The Mudaaf is the first noun — the possessed or the one attributed to another. E.g., in 'Khalid's book,' 'book' is Mudaaf.

question: What is the Mudaaf Ilayhi?
options: The possessor — second noun in idaafah
The adjective describing the first noun
explanation: Mudaaf Ilayhi is the second noun — the possessor. E.g., in 'Khalid's book,' Khalid is Mudaaf Ilayhi.

question: What ending does the Mudaaf (first noun) take?
explanation: The Mudaaf takes a single dammah (ُ). It drops both alif-laam AND tanween.

question: What ending does the Mudaaf Ilayhi (second noun) take when it has NO alif-laam?
explanation: Mudaaf Ilayhi takes two kasrahs (ٍ) — tanween kasrah — when it has no alif-laam. E.g., قَلَمُ خَالِدٍ.

question: Which statement about Mudaaf is correct?
options: It NEVER has alif-laam AND NEVER has tanween
explanation: Key rule: The Mudaaf NEVER accepts alif-laam AND NEVER accepts tanween. Both are forbidden.

question: If the Mudaaf Ilayhi has alif-laam (ال), its ending changes to...
explanation: When Mudaaf Ilayhi has alif-laam, tanween drops: two kasrahs become one kasrah. E.g., خَاتَمُ الذَّهَبِ.

question: In 'كِتَابُ الطَّالِبِ', what is the Mudaaf Ilayhi?
explanation: الطَّالِبِ is the Mudaaf Ilayhi — it ends with kasrah because it has alif-laam. كِتَابُ is the Mudaaf.

question: In 'قَلَمُ مَحْمُودٍ', what is the Mudaaf?
explanation: قَلَمُ (pen) is the Mudaaf — it has a single dammah and no tanween. مَحْمُودٍ is the Mudaaf Ilayhi.

question: What is the FIRST step to translate 'Fatimah's pen' into Arabic?
options: Remove the apostrophe-s and reverse the word order
explanation: For 'Fatimah's pen': remove 's → 'Fatimah pen' → reverse to 'pen Fatimah' → translate → apply endings: قَلَمُ فَاطِمَةَ.

question: Which is the correct Arabic for 'door of the house' (بَابُ الْبَيْتِ)? Why does الْبَيْتِ have one kasrah not two?
options: Because alif-laam on the Mudaaf Ilayhi causes tanween to drop to a single kasrah
It is a mistake — it should have two kasrahs
explanation: When Mudaaf Ilayhi has alif-laam, tanween (ٍ) drops to a single kasrah (ِ): الْبَيْتِ (not الْبَيْتٍ).

question: Which phrase correctly says 'the pen of the grandfather'?
explanation: قَلَمُ (Mudaaf — single dammah, no tanween, no alif-laam) + الْجَدِّ (Mudaaf Ilayhi — single kasrah because of alif-laam).

question: What relationship does the idaafah (Mudaaf-Mudaaf Ilayhi) construction typically express?
options: Possession or attribution of one noun to another
explanation: Idaafah expresses possession or attribution — 'the X of Y' or 'Y's X'. E.g., كِتَابُ خَالِدٍ = Khalid's book.

question: What does جِدَارٌ mean?
explanation: جِدَارٌ (jidārun) = wall. Plural: جُدْرَانٌ.

question: What does بَيْتٌ mean?
explanation: بَيْتٌ (baytun) = house. Plural: بُيُوتٌ. Another word for house/home is دَارٌ.

question: What does شُبَّاكٌ mean?
explanation: شُبَّاكٌ (shabbākun) = window. Plural: شَبَابِيكُ.

question: What does سَقْفٌ mean?
explanation: سَقْفٌ (saqfun) = ceiling. Plural: سُقُوفٌ. Roof is سَطْحٌ.

question: What does سَرِيرٌ mean?
explanation: سَرِيرٌ (sarīrun) = bed. Plural: سُرُرٌ.

question: What does قَلَمٌ mean?
explanation: قَلَمٌ (qalamun) = pen. Plural: أَقْلَامٌ.

question: What does ضَوْءٌ mean?
explanation: ضَوْءٌ (ḍaw'un) = light. Plural: أَضْوَاءٌ. Darkness is ظُلْمَةٌ.

question: What does ظُلْمَةٌ mean?
explanation: ظُلْمَةٌ (ẓulmatun) = darkness. Plural: ظُلُمَاتٌ. E.g., ظُلْمَةُ اللَّيْلِ = darkness of the night.

question: What does سِرَاجٌ mean?
explanation: سِرَاجٌ (sirājun) = lamp, lantern. Plural: سُرُجٌ.

question: What does صَلَاةٌ mean?
explanation: صَلَاةٌ (ṣalātun) = prayer, salah — one of the five pillars of Islam.

question: What does زَكَاةٌ mean?
explanation: زَكَاةٌ (zakātun) = obligatory alms/charity (zakah) — one of the five pillars of Islam.

question: What does مَدِينَةٌ mean?
explanation: مَدِينَةٌ (madīnatun) = city. Plural: مُدُنٌ.

question: What does شِتَاءٌ mean?
explanation: شِتَاءٌ (shitā'un) = winter. The four seasons: شِتَاءٌ (winter), رَبِيعٌ (spring), صَيْفٌ (summer), خَرِيفٌ (autumn).

question: What does صَيْفٌ mean?
explanation: صَيْفٌ (ṣayfun) = summer.

question: What does خَرِيفٌ mean?
explanation: خَرِيفٌ (kharīfun) = autumn, fall.

question: What does طَوِيلٌ mean?
explanation: طَوِيلٌ (ṭawīlun) = tall, long. Its opposite is قَصِيرٌ (short).

question: What does ثَمِينٌ mean?
explanation: ثَمِينٌ (thamīnun) = expensive, precious.

question: What does كُرَّاسَةٌ mean?
explanation: كُرَّاسَةٌ (kurrāsatun) = notebook, exercise book.

question: What does سَفَرٌ mean?
explanation: سَفَرٌ (safarun) = journey, travel. E.g., صَلَاةُ السَّفَرِ = the prayer of travel (shortened prayer).

question: What does أَمِيرٌ mean?
explanation: أَمِيرٌ (amīrun) = leader, governor, prince. E.g., أَمِيرُ الْمَدِينَةِ = leader of the city.

question: Translate: يَوْمُ الدِّينِ
explanation: يَوْمُ = day (Mudaaf) + الدِّينِ = of the religion/judgment (Mudaaf Ilayhi). Together: 'Day of Judgment.'

question: Translate: ظُلْمَةُ اللَّيْلِ
explanation: ظُلْمَةُ (darkness, Mudaaf) + اللَّيْلِ (of the night, Mudaaf Ilayhi with alif-laam → single kasrah).

question: Translate: أَمِيرُ الْمَدِينَةِ
options: The leader is in the city
explanation: أَمِيرُ (leader, Mudaaf) + الْمَدِينَةِ (of the city, Mudaaf Ilayhi) = 'leader of the city.'

question: Translate: إِقَامَةُ الصَّلَاةِ
explanation: إِقَامَةُ (establishing, Mudaaf) + الصَّلَاةِ (of the prayer, Mudaaf Ilayhi) = 'establishing of the prayer.'

question: Which is the correct Arabic for 'Mahmood's pen is expensive'?
explanation: قَلَمُ (Mudaaf — single dammah, no tanween) + مَحْمُودٍ (Mudaaf Ilayhi — two kasrahs) + ثَمِينٌ (Khabar). The whole phrase قَلَمُ مَحْمُودٍ acts as the Mubtada.

question: Translate: 'Days of summer are long.'
explanation: أَيَّامُ (days, plural Mudaaf) + الصَّيْفِ (of summer, Mudaaf Ilayhi) = Mubtada; طَوِيلَةٌ = Khabar (feminine because أَيَّام is treated as feminine plural).

question: Translate: ضَوْءُ سِرَاجٍ
explanation: ضَوْءُ (light, Mudaaf) + سِرَاجٍ (of a lamp — no alif-laam so two kasrahs) = 'light of a lamp.'

question: What does 'Sulasi Mujarrad' (سُلَاسِي مُجَرَّد) mean?
explanation: Sulasi Mujarrad means a bare three-letter root verb — the most common verb pattern in Arabic. Example: كَتَبَ (k-t-b), ذَهَبَ (dh-h-b).

question: In the pattern فَعَلَ, what does each letter (ف، ع، ل) represent?
options: They are the actual letters of a verb
They are placeholders for the 3 root letters
explanation: فَعَلَ is a template: ف = 1st root letter, ع = 2nd, ل = 3rd. Replace them with any 3-letter verb, e.g. كَتَبَ replaces ف→ك، ع→ت، ل→ب.

question: Which term means 'Third Person' (he/she/they) in Arabic grammar?
explanation: الغَائِب (Al-Ghaaib) means 'the absent one' — he/she/they — the person being talked ABOUT.

question: Which term means 'Second Person' (you) in Arabic grammar?
explanation: المُخَاطَب (Al-Mukhaatab) means 'the one being addressed' — you. The person you are talking TO.

question: Which term means 'First Person' (I/we) in Arabic grammar?
explanation: المُتَكَلِّم (Al-Mutakallim) means 'the speaker' — I or we. The person who is speaking.

question: What is the past tense form of كَتَبَ for هُوَ (he)?
explanation: هُوَ (he) uses the base form: كَتَبَ. This is the dictionary/root form — no suffix added.

question: What is the past tense form of ذَهَبَ for هِيَ (she)?
explanation: هِيَ (she) adds تْ to the base verb: ذَهَبَتْ. The تْ (ta marboota) marks the feminine 3rd person singular.

question: What is the past tense form of كَتَبَ for هُمْ (they, masculine plural)?
explanation: هُمْ (they, masculine plural) uses فَعَلُوا pattern: كَتَبُوا. The وا suffix marks masculine plural 3rd person.

question: What suffix is added to the verb for هُمَا (they two, masculine dual)?
explanation: For هُمَا masculine dual, add ا (alif): فَعَلَا. Trick: dual masculine 3rd person always ends in ā (alif).

question: What is the past tense form of ذَهَبَ for أَنْتَ (you, masculine singular)?
explanation: أَنْتَ (you, m sg) uses فَعَلْتَ pattern: ذَهَبْتَ. Note the sukoon on ب and فتحة on تَ.

question: What is the difference between فَعَلْتَ (anta) and فَعَلْتِ (anti)?
options: No difference, they mean the same
فَعَلْتَ is for 'you (masculine)' and فَعَلْتِ is for 'you (feminine)'
فَعَلْتَ is plural and فَعَلْتِ is singular
فَعَلْتَ is 3rd person and فَعَلْتِ is 2nd person
explanation: The only difference is the vowel on تَ vs تِ — fatha (ـَ) for masculine (أَنْتَ), kasra (ـِ) for feminine (أَنْتِ). This is a key distinction!

question: What is the past tense form of كَتَبَ for أَنَا (I)?
explanation: أَنَا (I) uses فَعَلْتُ pattern: كَتَبْتُ. Note the damma (ُ) on تُ — this distinguishes 'I' from 'you (m)' which has a fatha.

question: What is the past tense form of ذَهَبَ for نَحْنُ (we)?
explanation: نَحْنُ (we) uses فَعَلْنَا pattern: ذَهَبْنَا. Easy trick: نَحْنُ → نَا ending.

question: Which suffix marks the 3rd person feminine plural (هُنَّ)?
explanation: هُنَّ (they, feminine plural) uses the نَ suffix: فَعَلْنَ. Also note sukoon on the ل before نَ.

question: What is a key trick to identify 2nd and 1st person past tense forms?
options: They all contain the letter تَ/تِ/تُ
They all have alif at the end
They all use هُوَ as base
explanation: All 2nd person (أَنْتَ، أَنْتِ، أَنْتُمَا، أَنْتُمْ، أَنْتُنَّ) and 1st person (أَنَا) forms contain the letter تَ/تِ/تُ. Look for ت and you know it's 2nd or 1st person!

question: What does كَتَبَ mean?
explanation: كَتَبَ (kataba) means 'he wrote'. Root: ك-ت-ب. You'll see this root in كِتَابٌ (book) and كَاتِبٌ (writer).

question: What does ذَهَبَ mean?
explanation: ذَهَبَ (dhahaba) means 'he went'. Common verb you'll see constantly in Arabic texts.

question: What does a preposition (حَرْفُ جَرٍّ) do to the noun following it?
explanation: Prepositions cause the following noun to become Majroor — ending with kasrah (ِ) or two kasrahs (ٍ).

question: Which preposition means 'in' or 'inside'?
explanation: فِي means 'in' or 'inside'. E.g., فِي الْبَيْتِ = in the house.

question: What is the attached pronoun for 'his' when added to a noun?
explanation: ـهُ (-hu) is the attached pronoun for 'his'. E.g., كِتَابُهُ = his book.

question: What is the Arabic detached pronoun for 'we'?
explanation: نَحْنُ (naḥnu) = we. First person plural detached pronoun.

question: Which prefix does NOT belong to the present tense prefixes (أَنَيْتُ)?
explanation: سَ is a future marker, not a present tense prefix. The four are: أَ، نَ، يَ، تَ.

question: What is يَنْصُرُ in passive voice (Majhool)?
explanation: Passive present: change prefix vowel to dammah AND vowel before last letter to fathah → يُنْصَرُ.

question: In Arabic, where does the adjective (Sifa) come relative to the noun it describes?
explanation: In Arabic the adjective (Sifa) ALWAYS comes AFTER the noun — opposite to English.

question: Which phrase correctly means 'the righteous man' in Arabic?
explanation: الرَّجُلُ الصَّالِحُ — noun first, then adjective, both definite with alif-laam.

question: How is the prohibitive (Nahy) formed in Arabic?
options: لَا + 2nd person present tense in jazm form
Remove the prefix from the present tense
Add مَا before the past tense
explanation: Prohibitive: لَا + 2nd person present tense verb in jazm (sukoon) form. E.g., لَا تَذْهَبْ = Don't go!

question: What is the imperative (command) form of يَذْهَبُ for أَنْتَ?
explanation: Remove تَ prefix from تَذْهَبُ → ذْهَبْ → add connecting hamza → اِذْهَبْ.

question: How is the dual (Muthanna) formed in the Rafa' state?
explanation: Dual in Rafa': add ـَانِ to the singular. E.g., طَالِبٌ → طَالِبَانِ (two students).

question: What is the broken plural of كِتَابٌ (book)?
explanation: كُتُبٌ is the broken plural of كِتَابٌ — broken plurals change internal vowel patterns.

question: What is the correct word order in an Arabic verbal sentence?
explanation: Arabic verbal sentences follow Verb (فِعْلٌ) then Subject (فَاعِلٌ) then Object (مَفْعُولٌ).

question: What case ending does the فَاعِلٌ (subject) take?
explanation: The فَاعِلٌ is in the rafa state and always takes dammah or tanween dammah.

question: What case ending does the مَفْعُولٌ (object) take?
explanation: The مَفْعُولٌ is in the nasb state and always takes fathah or tanween fathah.

question: What is the meaning of فَاعِلٌ?
explanation: فَاعِلٌ means the subject or doer — the person performing the action.

question: What is the meaning of مَفْعُولٌ?
explanation: مَفْعُولٌ means the object — the person or thing the action is done to.

question: In the sentence نَصَرَ حَامِدٌ مَحْمُودًا, which word is the فَاعِلٌ?
explanation: حَامِدٌ has two dammahs (tanween dammah) so it is the فَاعِلٌ (doer).

question: In the sentence نَصَرَ حَامِدٌ مَحْمُودًا, which word is the مَفْعُولٌ?
explanation: مَحْمُودًا has two fathahs (tanween fathah) so it is the مَفْعُولٌ (receiver).

question: When alif-laam (ال) is added to a word, what happens to tanween?
options: Tanween drops to a single harakah
explanation: Alif-laam drops tanween to a single harakah: خَادِمٌ becomes الْخَادِمُ.

question: How do you say "The servant opened the door" in Arabic?
explanation: With alif-laam on both words: فَتَحَ الْخَادِمُ الْبَابَ. Tanween drops to a single harakah.

question: When a mudaaf acts as a faail or mafool, where does the case ending appear?
options: On both mudaaf and mudaaf ilayhi
explanation: The case ending shows on the mudaaf alone. The mudaaf ilayhi always stays in jarr state.

question: What does نَصَرَ mean?
explanation: نَصَرَ means he helped.

question: What does كَتَبَ mean?
explanation: كَتَبَ means he wrote.

question: What does ضَرَبَ mean?
explanation: ضَرَبَ means he hit or he beat.

question: What does سَمِعَ mean?
explanation: سَمِعَ means he heard.

question: Which verb means "he worshipped"?
explanation: عَبَدَ means he worshipped.

question: What does قَرَأَ mean?
explanation: قَرَأَ means he read.

question: What does جَعَلَ mean?
explanation: جَعَلَ means he made (someone into someone or something into something).
